issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.27-devel_5aa006d Thu Jan 29 16:48:45 2026 -0800 linux/amd64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='gcc'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='g++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOAMD64='v1'
GOARCH='amd64'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/home/denk/.cache/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/home/denk/.config/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -pthread -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build2520897468=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches'
GOHOSTARCH='amd64'
GOHOSTOS='linux'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/dev/null'
GOMODCACHE='/home/denk/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='linux'
GOPATH='/home/denk/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/home/denk/sdk/gotip'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='off'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/home/denk/.config/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/home/denk/sdk/gotip/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.27-devel_5aa006d Thu Jan 29 16:48:45 2026 -0800'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

Compile the following program with `-gcflags="-S"` and search for `runtime.panicBounds`:

```go
package main

func Range(n int) []int {
	m := make([]int, n)

	for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
		m[i] = i // runtime.panicBounds
	}

	for i := range n {
		m[i] = i // runtime.panicBounds
	}

	for i := range len(m) {
		m[i] = i
	}

	for i := range m {
		m[i] = i
	}

	return m
}

func F(size int) {
	Range(size)
}

func main() {
	F(-1)
}
```

### What did you see happen?

When `Range` is inlined (default behavior without `//go:noinline`), the bounds checks remain in the generated assembly:

```bash
$ go build -gcflags="-S" main.go 2>&1 | grep runtime.panicBounds
```

```
  0x00a0 00160 (/main.go:12) CALL  runtime.panicBounds(SB)
  0x00a5 00165 (/main.go:8)  CALL  runtime.panicBounds(SB)
  rel 161+4 t=R_CALL runtime.panicBounds+0
  rel 166+4 t=R_CALL runtime.panicBounds+0
```

However, when we add `//go:noinline` to the `Range` function:

```go
//go:noinline
func Range(n int) []int {
	// ...
}
```

The bounds checks are properly eliminated by the compiler:

```bash
$ go build -gcflags="-S" main.go 2>&1 | grep runtime.panicBounds
# No runtime.panicBounds calls
```

### What did you expect to see?

When `Range` is inlined into `F`, the bounds check elimination (BCE) optimizations should work the same way as when the function is not inlined. The compiler should recognize that:
1. `m` has length `n`
2. Loop indices `i` are guaranteed to be within bounds `[0, n)`
3. Therefore, bounds checks on `m[i]` can be eliminated

```
#!watchflakes
default <- pkg == "runtime" && test == "TestFinalizerOrCleanupDeadlock/Cleanup/Panic"
```

Issue created automatically to collect these failures.

Example ([log](https://ci.chromium.org/b/8691501682900345985)):

    === RUN   TestFinalizerOrCleanupDeadlock/Cleanup/Panic
    === PAUSE TestFinalizerOrCleanupDeadlock/Cleanup/Panic
    === CONT  TestFinalizerOrCleanupDeadlock/Cleanup/Panic
        crash_test.go:1181: /home/swarming/.swarming/w/ir/x/t/go-build3427023277/testprog.exe CleanupDeadlock: exit status 2
        crash_test.go:1184: output does not contain "runtime.runCleanups()":
            panic: panic
            
            goroutine 1 [running]:
            main.FinalizerOrCleanupDeadlock(0x1)
            	/home/swarming/.swarming/w/ir/x/w/goroot/src/runtime/testdata/testprog/finalizer_deadlock.go:48 +0x6ab
            main.init.8.func2()
            	/home/swarming/.swarming/w/ir/x/w/goroot/src/runtime/testdata/testprog/finalizer_deadlock.go:19 +0x1e
            main.main()
            	/home/swarming/.swarming/w/ir/x/w/goroot/src/runtime/testdata/testprog/main.go:34 +0x14e
            
            goroutine 19 [running]:

issue.title: cmd/go: builds appear slower on openbsd/amd64 with Go 1.26rc2 (vs 1.25.6)

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
When Go 1.26rc2 was being tested in the OpenBSD ports tree, it was noticed that Grafana was building slower than it had previously (with Go 1.25.6). Since the build machinery is building a large volume of packages, limitations are in place to prevent builds from trying to take over the entire machine - part of this appears to be (understandably) aggravated by `GOMAXPROCS=1`, however even increasing this to 2 or 4, there is still a seemingly ~5% slowdown in build time:

With -p=1 and GOMAXPROCS=1:

```
1.25.6:

   15m31.78s real    11m18.22s user     2m45.24s system                                                                                                                                                                                        

1.26rc2

   18m18.46s real    13m46.76s user     2m57.95s system
```

With -p=2 and GOMAXPROCS=2:

```
1.25.6:

   10m11.78s real    19m07.66s user     5m05.13s system

1.26rc2:

   10m38.15s real    19m31.14s user     5m27.86s system
```

With -p=2 and GOMAXPROCS=4:

```
1.25.6:

    9m21.32s real    23m38.35s user     7m13.84s system

1.26rc2:

    9m56.93s real    24m53.99s user     7m44.58s system
```

I've not had time to try to trace this further, or verify on other environments (not sure if it is potentially expected). Figured it was worth reporting all the same.

issue.title: cmd/compile: optimize string concatenation to avoid runtime calls

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: FeatureRequest
labels.description: Issues asking for a new feature that does not need a proposal.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

milestone.title: Unplanned
milestone.description: No plan to fix in any specific release.

issue.body: Hi team, I've identified a potential optimization opportunity to eliminate concat calls for constant strings and replace them directly with a LEAQ straddr instruction, similar to what the [Java JIT](https://github.com/openjdk/jdk/blob/master/src/hotspot/share/opto/stringopts.hpp) does. This is a cheap optimization that can enable further optimizations down the line, such as compile-time evaluation of len(const_string). A local run of .`/make.bash` shows 200+ potential sites for this optimization. Here is an example: ```go func ff(a, b int) string { y := "abc" t := y + "def" if t == "abcdef" { return t + "ghi" } return t } ``` before: after applying opts: For implementation, we could either - add a dedicated [stringopt pass](https://github.com/y1yang0/go/commit/2f74a406d7ff72255a995b6120129ffc26c735af) before expand_call (which centralizes the code for future optimizations like strcmp) or - integrate it into the [sccp pass](https://github.com/y1yang0/go/commit/d08577e5aa5185831715c19473fab49f57a0488d) after expand_call (which uses its framework but disperses the code) or - add some generic rules Do you have a preference on the approach? Would the community be interested in this?

issue.title: runtime: garbage collector panic

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

1.24.9 to 1.25.5

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
- OS: Linux
- Architecture: amd64 and arm64
- Environment: containerized (k8s)
- All kinds of large instance types on various cloud providers: c2d-standard-56, n2d-standard-32, standard_d32as_v5, c7g.12xlarge, m7g.16xlarge
```

### What did you do?

A Go service is hitting a garbage collector panic in internal deployments.

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
```

I know that it was already happening when we used Go 1.24.9, and it is still happening with 1.25.5. I don't have older logs available so I don't know when it actually started.

I don't have a reproducible example but it's happening 10~20 times a day across our prod environment, and I can deploy a custom version / build with a custom Go if needed to collect information.

I checked all Go memstats metrics and some host metrics of an occurence and didn't see anything remarkable, it was not that close to the memory limits, not allocating that much, not using a lot of CPU, etc.

I'm pretty sure we don't set GOMEMLIMIT, we set GOGC to 50, and one occurence had GOMAXPROCS set to 21 (but that can vary a lot depending on the host).

If it changes anything the service is privileged and uses eBPF for system monitoring. The entrypoint is defined [here](https://github.com/DataDog/datadog-agent/blob/main/cmd/system-probe/main.go), but it's not a trivial binary.

### What did you see happen?

Here is the start of a stack trace of an occurrence, I can share the full stack trace if it's useful.

<details>
<summary>Stacktrace</summary>

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
runtime stack:
runtime.throw({0x2890e61?, 0x2c8be90?})
	runtime/panic.go:1094 +0x48 fp=0x74aa367fbba0 sp=0x74aa367fbb70 pc=0x48b008
panic({0x22eac60?, 0x2c8be90?})
	runtime/panic.go:747 +0x33b fp=0x74aa367fbc50 sp=0x74aa367fbba0 pc=0x48addb
runtime.gcMark(0x4956cd?)
	runtime/mgc.go:1633 +0x409 fp=0x74aa367fbcc8 sp=0x74aa367fbc50 pc=0x4327a9
runtime.gcMarkTermination.func1()
	runtime/mgc.go:1059 +0x17 fp=0x74aa367fbce0 sp=0x74aa367fbcc8 pc=0x431b57
runtime.systemstack(0x800000)
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:513 +0x4a fp=0x74aa367fbcf0 sp=0x74aa367fbce0 pc=0x491d0a
goroutine 29 gp=0xc0003936c0 m=71 mp=0xc004181808 [garbage collection]:
runtime.systemstack_switch()
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:478 +0x8 fp=0xc003f1fa28 sp=0xc003f1fa18 pc=0x491ca8
runtime.gcMarkTermination({0x1?, 0x12401ba40c627?, 0x12401ba411b63?, 0x2f6b3?})
	runtime/mgc.go:1058 +0x135 fp=0xc003f1fe98 sp=0xc003f1fa28 pc=0x430a35
runtime.gcMarkDone()
	runtime/mgc.go:1032 +0x369 fp=0xc003f1ff38 sp=0xc003f1fe98 pc=0x4306a9
runtime.gcBgMarkWorker(0xc000066930)
	runtime/mgc.go:1600 +0x345 fp=0xc003f1ffc8 sp=0xc003f1ff38 pc=0x432085
runtime.gcBgMarkStartWorkers.gowrap1()
	runtime/mgc.go:1373 +0x25 fp=0xc003f1ffe0 sp=0xc003f1ffc8 pc=0x431d05
runtime.goexit({})
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:1693 +0x1 fp=0xc003f1ffe8 sp=0xc003f1ffe0 pc=0x493cc1
created by runtime.gcBgMarkStartWorkers in goroutine 1
	runtime/mgc.go:1373 +0x105
```

</details>

### What did you expect to see?

The garbage collector should complete without panicking.

issue.body:
### Go version

https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/sys/unix 0.40.0

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
N/A
```

### What did you do?

I am looking for any way to call ioctl BLKGETSIZE64 that is not just manually calling syscall,
and there does not seem to be anything.

Maybe a

IoctlGetUint64(fd int, req uint) (uint64, error)

could be implemented in x/sys/unix
that would cover all syscalls similar to BLKGETSIZE64 which return an uint64, similarly to:

[func IoctlGetUint32(fd int, req uint) (uint32, error)](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/sys/unix#IoctlGetUint32)



### What did you see happen?

No IoctlGetUint64 is found or any other similar ioctl variant

### What did you expect to see?

I expected to find an IoctlGetUint64, similarly to what is provided for IoctlGetUint32

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
GOVERSION='go1.26rc2'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='/home/george/Dev/openwrt-openwrt/staging_dir/host/bin/pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

Compiling target Go with host Go using the OpenWrt 25.12-rc3 toolchain with Spectre mitigations enabled for amd64 through:

```
-gcflags "all=-spectre all" -asmflags "all=-spectre all"
```

Both host and target are 1.26rc2.

Full host Go command list:

```bash
export
	GOOS="linux"
	GOARCH="amd64"
	GO386=""
	GOAMD64="v1"
	GOARM=""
	GOARM64=""
	GOMIPS=""
	GOMIPS64=""
	GOPPC64=""
	CGO_ENABLED=1 CC="x86_64-openwrt-linux-musl-gcc"
	CXX="x86_64-openwrt-linux-musl-g++"
	CGO_CFLAGS="-Os -pipe -fno-caller-saves -fno-plt -fhonour-copts -fmacro-prefix-map=/path_to_build_dir/target-x86_64_musl/go-1.26.0=go-1.26.0 -Wformat -Werror=format-security -fstack-protector -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=1 -Wl,-z,now -Wl,-z,relro -Wl,-z,pack-relative-relocs"
	CGO_CPPFLAGS="-I/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/usr/include -I/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/include -I/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/include/fortify"
	CGO_CXXFLAGS="-Os -pipe -fno-caller-saves -fno-plt -fhonour-copts -fmacro-prefix-map=/path_to_build_dir/target-x86_64_musl/go-1.26.0=go-1.26.0 -Wformat -Werror=format-security -fstack-protector -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=1 -Wl,-z,now -Wl,-z,relro -Wl,-z,pack-relative-relocs"
	CGO_LDFLAGS="-L/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/usr/lib -L/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/lib -fuse-ld=bfd -znow -zrelro -zpack-relative-relocs"

GO_GCC_HELPER_CC="x86_64-openwrt-linux-musl-gcc"
GO_GCC_HELPER_CXX="x86_64-openwrt-linux-musl-g++"
GOHOSTARCH="amd64"
GOCACHE="/path_to_tmp/go-build"
GOENV=off
GO_GCC_HELPER_PATH="$PATH"
CC=gcc CXX=g++
PKG_CONFIG=pkg-config
PATH="/path_to_staging_dir/hostpkg/lib/go-1.26/openwrt:$PATH"
go install
	-a
	-buildvcs=false
	-trimpath
	-ldflags "all=-buildid '1769552642' -linkmode external -extldflags '-L/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/usr/lib -L/path_to_staging_dir/toolchain-x86_64_gcc-14.3.0_musl/lib -fuse-ld=bfd -Wl,-z,now -Wl,-z,relro -Wl,-z,pack-relative-relocs' -s -w"
	-gcflags "all=-spectre all"
	-asmflags "all=-spectre all" std cmd
```

Exports and commands are separated into multiple lines without back slashes for readability.

### What did you see happen?

Target compilation fails with:

```bash
# internal/runtime/startlinetest
asm: non-retpoline-compatible: 00005 (/path_to_go_dir/src/internal/runtime/startlinetest/func_amd64.s:27)	CALL	(DX)
asm: assembly failed
# internal/runtime/gc/scan
asm: non-retpoline-compatible: 00005 (/path_to_go_dir/src/internal/runtime/gc/scan/scan_amd64.s:15)	CALL	(BX)(CX*8)
asm: non-retpoline-compatible: 00015 (/path_to_go_dir/src/internal/runtime/gc/scan/scan_amd64.s:28)	CALL	(BX)(CX*8)
asm: assembly failed
```

Same thing sans the `scan_amd64.s` part happens on 1.25.6.

### What did you expect to see?

Target Go compiles without errors with Spectre mitigations enabled.

comment.body:
Oh oops, looks like we both sent changes. I'm planning to add a smoke test to `cmd/dist` in my change as well, as a start; that might be easier for me at this point.

One reason to add a smoke test on _all_ builders in addition to a dedicated builder is that a dedicated builder may not actually run a lot of the instrumented assembly. For example, we have an AVX-512 builder.

issue.title: proposal: new `Recorder` types in runtime/pprof

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

comment.body:
> I personally don't think any of those options are great, and panicking in particular is probably off the table. If we truly want to support multiple independent consumers, maybe we just bite the bullet and support different sampling rates?

I agree. Supporting different sampling rates is definitely ideal, and would be the best thing for everyone. FTR, if you're willing to try it out and implement it, I'm 100% for it.

That being said, even though those options aren't great, I'm generally okay with having the "old world" get more messy. Your comment makes me realize that what this proposal is missing is a smooth path forward to the "new world." If you try this out and decide it's just way too much effort to tackle to start with, then I think we can work on figuring out how to migrate people over more smoothly.

issue.title: x/exp/trace,internal/trace: support event writing

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

comment.body:
> We discussed the proposal in [#57175 (comment)](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/57175#issuecomment-3819297694). Brief summary of some of the feedback:
> 
> * The short, straightforward API is quite nice.

+1

> * Allowing the trace version is helpful for the testing use case, so you can test that traces from old versions of Go work properly. On the other hand, the reader doesn't expose the trace version, so the two APIs are inconsistent.

I think we should expose the trace version. I don't know yet whether we should make the `version` package public though. I think we should consider having a more opaque wrapper type.

> * How much validation should this API do that you are writing a valid trace? Validation likely makes it easier to use, but also adds complexity.

Since it's possible to create events from scratch, I would argue we should perform full validation (I don't see much of a use-case for writing an invalid trace for what Felix has in mind). I don't think validation is _too_ complicated because everything is already serialized. I think it's just matter of maintaining a few maps to make sure no states are inconsistent. It should at least be a _lot_ easier than validating on the reader side.

> * Should the implementation be able to use unexported fields of the Event? This may help keep the API simple for filtering use cases, but also may mean it is impossible to construct some traces other than by getting Events from a reader of an existing trace.

I think we should move toward a world where accessing the unexported fields doesn't matter. If there's an event we can't construct, we should make it possible to construct it. Happy to help figure out what it is that's not possible to construct and iron that over.

issue.title: unique: Handle memory leaks; cleanup goroutine can't keep up

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body: ### Go version `go version go1.24.0 darwin/arm64` `go version go1.24.0 linux/amd64` `go version go1.23.6 darwin/arm64` `go version go1.23.6 linux/amd64` ### What did you do? We have a server that processes a stream of user connect & disconnect events from our edge servers. Users are identified by a `[32]byte` public key, wrapped up like: ```go type NodePublic struct { k [32]byte } ``` This server maintains ~4 maps keyed by `NodePublic`. Out of curiosity and as an experiment, we decided to kick the tires with `unique.Handle` to make those maps instead be keyed by `unique.Handle[NodePublic]` to make them 8 bytes instead of 32. It saves about 96 bytes per connection. Not a ton, but ... an experiment. The experiment didn't go as expected. ### What did you see happen? What we saw happen was unbounded memory growth and kernel OOMs. These are the two instances of this server we run. (it's an internal debug tool only, so we only run two for HA reasons) It previously took about ~1GB of memory at steady state. With `unique.Handle`, it just keeps growing until the kernel OOM kills it. The flat parts in the graph are where we changed the map key types from `unique.Handle[NodePublic]` back to just `NodePublic`, changing nothing else. To debug, I hacked up the `unique` package (patch: https://gist.github.com/bradfitz/06f57b1515d58fb4d8b9b4eb5a84262e) to try out two things: * see if the weak pointers were getting tinyalloc'ed and preventing GC. To counter this, I tried making the `unique` package use 24 byte value types in its uniqueMap. No change. * to log stats when the cleaners ran to see how much it tried to delete * Oh, and I also tried both Go 1.23.6 and Go 1.24.0 The result is that never frees anything. The weak pointer is never `nil`. It takes ever longer to iterate over the map (up to 2.7 seconds now), never finding anything to delete: ``` Feb 16 00:30:44 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 16µs Feb 16 00:30:46 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2829766 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2829766 ; took 2.585244s Feb 16 00:32:51 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 14µs Feb 16 00:32:53 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2850211 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2850211 ; took 2.692595s Feb 16 00:34:58 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 19µs Feb 16 00:35:01 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2870410 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2870410 ; took 2.728681s Feb 16 00:37:06 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 19µs Feb 16 00:37:08 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2890817 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2890817 ; took 2.635071s Feb 16 00:39:13 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 18µs Feb 16 00:39:16 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2911098 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2911098 ; took 2.600478s Feb 16 00:41:20 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 19µs Feb 16 00:41:22 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2931399 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2931399 ; took 2.606853s Feb 16 00:43:27 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2 ; took 20µs Feb 16 00:43:29 derptrackd2 derptrackd[771925]: XXX did unique cleanup loop: items= 2951574 ; deleted= 0 ; kept= 2951574 ; took 2.701304s ``` Looking at historical logs, it never deletes anything on Linux. I tried to write a repro (on my Mac) and I failed, despite a bunch of efforts. I ended up doing too much debugging on a remote AWS VM, with a hacked up Go toolchain. Eventually I ran the same code on my Mac (same server + same hacked up Go toolchain), pulling from the same data sources, and surprisingly: it worked! `unique` finds weak things to delete in its cleaner and it doesn't grow. In summary: * `unique` leaks on `linux/amd64` (prod cloud VM) * same code on same data doesn't leak on `darwin/arm64` (my dev laptop) I'll now try revisiting my standalone repro but running it on Linux instead of macOS. I had hoped to deliver @mknyszek a bug on a silver platter before filing, but I'm going a little crazy at this pointed and figured it was time to start sharing. ### What did you expect to see? No memory leaks on Linux also.

comment.body:
I think you're going to need to provide more details.

I did try to fix it, but as of https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71772#issuecomment-2824915713, the fix was never validated, and I don't have another (useful, real-world) reproducer at the moment. (It's still possible to out-run the cleanup goroutines if you really try, but it should be much harder to do it than it used to be. The situation with `unique` specifically should be orders of magnitude better.) I think I'm going to need more data to keep iterating on this.

(For example, it's possible that the reason this *isn't* fixed is because in my fix I was conservative with how many cleanup goroutines we're willing to spin up, so that might be something to try. Change `gomaxprocs/4` to `gomaxprocs` here if you want to try something: https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/master:src/runtime/mcleanup.go;l=709;drc=eb63ef9d6676dc0edb112cd297820306a327017a)

issue.title: runtime: expose number of running/runnable goroutines

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: early-in-cycle
labels.description: A change that should be done early in the 3 month dev cycle.

milestone.title: Unplanned
milestone.description: No plan to fix in any specific release.

issue.body:
### Summary

I'd like to propose a way to expose the number of active (running + runnable) goroutines.
### Background

My primary use case for this metric is to estimate application load (`num-active-goroutines / num-cpu`) in order to implement load shedding. Other metrics, like the `times()` syscall, don't expose application overload and don't work well in the presence of noisy neighbours.
### Plan

Currently the runtime package includes `runtime.NumGoroutine() int` which returns the number of live, non-system goroutines.

The runtime package could be extended to include `runtime.NumActiveGoroutine() int`. `NumActiveGoroutine()` should count all goroutines where `isSystemGoroutine()` is false and where status is `_Grunnable|_Grunning|_Gsyscall`.

It seems that such a function would need to acquire `sched.lock` and `allglock`. This could have some performance implications.

issue.title: runtime: garbage collector panic

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

1.24.9 to 1.25.5

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
- OS: Linux
- Architecture: amd64 and arm64
- Environment: containerized (k8s)
- All kinds of large instance types on various cloud providers: c2d-standard-56, n2d-standard-32, standard_d32as_v5, c7g.12xlarge, m7g.16xlarge
```

### What did you do?

A Go service is hitting a garbage collector panic in internal deployments.

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
```

I know that it was already happening when we used Go 1.24.9, and it is still happening with 1.25.5. I don't have older logs available so I don't know when it actually started.

I don't have a reproducible example but it's happening 10~20 times a day across our prod environment, and I can deploy a custom version / build with a custom Go if needed to collect information.

I checked all Go memstats metrics and some host metrics of an occurence and didn't see anything remarkable, it was not that close to the memory limits, not allocating that much, not using a lot of CPU, etc.

I'm pretty sure we don't set GOMEMLIMIT, we set GOGC to 50, and one occurence had GOMAXPROCS set to 21 (but that can vary a lot depending on the host).

If it changes anything the service is privileged and uses eBPF for system monitoring. The entrypoint is defined [here](https://github.com/DataDog/datadog-agent/blob/main/cmd/system-probe/main.go), but it's not a trivial binary.

### What did you see happen?

Here is the start of a stack trace of an occurrence, I can share the full stack trace if it's useful.

<details>
<summary>Stacktrace</summary>

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
runtime stack:
runtime.throw({0x2890e61?, 0x2c8be90?})
	runtime/panic.go:1094 +0x48 fp=0x74aa367fbba0 sp=0x74aa367fbb70 pc=0x48b008
panic({0x22eac60?, 0x2c8be90?})
	runtime/panic.go:747 +0x33b fp=0x74aa367fbc50 sp=0x74aa367fbba0 pc=0x48addb
runtime.gcMark(0x4956cd?)
	runtime/mgc.go:1633 +0x409 fp=0x74aa367fbcc8 sp=0x74aa367fbc50 pc=0x4327a9
runtime.gcMarkTermination.func1()
	runtime/mgc.go:1059 +0x17 fp=0x74aa367fbce0 sp=0x74aa367fbcc8 pc=0x431b57
runtime.systemstack(0x800000)
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:513 +0x4a fp=0x74aa367fbcf0 sp=0x74aa367fbce0 pc=0x491d0a
goroutine 29 gp=0xc0003936c0 m=71 mp=0xc004181808 [garbage collection]:
runtime.systemstack_switch()
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:478 +0x8 fp=0xc003f1fa28 sp=0xc003f1fa18 pc=0x491ca8
runtime.gcMarkTermination({0x1?, 0x12401ba40c627?, 0x12401ba411b63?, 0x2f6b3?})
	runtime/mgc.go:1058 +0x135 fp=0xc003f1fe98 sp=0xc003f1fa28 pc=0x430a35
runtime.gcMarkDone()
	runtime/mgc.go:1032 +0x369 fp=0xc003f1ff38 sp=0xc003f1fe98 pc=0x4306a9
runtime.gcBgMarkWorker(0xc000066930)
	runtime/mgc.go:1600 +0x345 fp=0xc003f1ffc8 sp=0xc003f1ff38 pc=0x432085
runtime.gcBgMarkStartWorkers.gowrap1()
	runtime/mgc.go:1373 +0x25 fp=0xc003f1ffe0 sp=0xc003f1ffc8 pc=0x431d05
runtime.goexit({})
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:1693 +0x1 fp=0xc003f1ffe8 sp=0xc003f1ffe0 pc=0x493cc1
created by runtime.gcBgMarkStartWorkers in goroutine 1
	runtime/mgc.go:1373 +0x105
```

</details>

### What did you expect to see?

The garbage collector should complete without panicking.

comment.body:
Thanks for the context. :) `crypto` and/or `net/netip` could be enough, but it depends on how far back it goes I guess. At least I know I can't rule `weak.Pointer` stuff out.

One thing we can try is to test if the problem is transient. We can do this by forcing a retry of the mark phase if we find that `work.full != 0`, same way we do for the local queues.

If the problem is transient, then the crashes should go away. You'll have more STWs, but your program will stop crashing. It's not a true fix, but it's a signal that there's some kind of race we're not accounting for. This suggests closer scrutiny at things like `weak.Pointer`.

On the other hand, if the problem is persistent, then your crashes will turn into _hangs_ that look like a lot of spinning without progress. This is the best-case scenario, since we should be able to add instrumentation and find exactly where the problem is happening. It suggests that you're managing to tickle some kind of issue directly in the mark termination algorithm itself. Unfortunately I suspect such an issue is probably also the least likely outcome. (We probably would've noticed it in our regular testing.)

comment.body:
> Green Tea shouldn't have anything to do with this. 

Ugh, actually, if the check that's failing is not a root job (unlikely) but the `work.full != 0` check, then yeah, Green Tea might _appear_ to fix the issue since global work queue for small objects with Green Tea anymore. However, Green Tea likely would not have truly fixed any such issues, only hidden them, because `work.full` still exists and is used, but is exclusively for larger objects. So even if it works with Go 1.26 RC2, I don't think we should consider it fixed without understanding the issue for that reason but there's an even worse potential issue...

> Well... the mark termination conditions changed slightly, but everything still follows the same fallback paths. To not take those fallback paths and end up here anyway requires a very specific set of circumstances...

For some additional context, "specific set of circumstances" means "no work in local queues, yet still somehow a piece of global work was missed." Green Tea's small object span queues are _only_ local, which means if the problem still exists with Green Tea but _appears_ to go away, it might just be [translating the crash into an inefficiency (a mark restart)](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/master:src/runtime/mgc.go;l=1107;drc=1bd5dbfc4110740f2126e0253f429dfe5f3d04ac).

This would be pretty bad because we'd have very little insight into the issue, and now we'll be carrying around a performance bug. (We should really fix https://github.com/golang/go/issues/27993, but it'll probably take a good bit of effort to prove (in the academic sense) that the fix really works. 😞 We accept the workaround because we have many reasons to believe it's extremely rare for the workaround to fire, but there may be a new bug here.)

I checked all Go memstats metrics and some host metrics of an occurence and didn't see anything remarkable, it was not that close to the memory limits, not allocating that much, not using a lot of CPU, etc.

I'm pretty sure we don't set GOMEMLIMIT, we set GOGC to 50, and one occurence had GOMAXPROCS set to 21 (but that can vary a lot depending on the host).

If it changes anything the service is privileged and uses eBPF for system monitoring. The entrypoint is defined [here](https://github.com/DataDog/datadog-agent/blob/main/cmd/system-probe/main.go), but it's not a trivial binary.

### What did you see happen?

Here is the start of a stack trace of an occurrence, I can share the full stack trace if it's useful.

<details>
<summary>Stacktrace</summary>


issue.title: runtime: garbage collector panic

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport

issue.body:
### Go version

1.24.9 to 1.25.5

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
- OS: Linux
- Architecture: amd64 and arm64
- Environment: containerized (k8s)
- All kinds of large instance types on various cloud providers: c2d-standard-56, n2d-standard-32, standard_d32as_v5, c7g.12xlarge, m7g.16xlarge
```

### What did you do?

A Go service is hitting a garbage collector panic in internal deployments.

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
```

I know that it was already happening when we used Go 1.24.9, and it is still happening with 1.25.5. I don't have older logs available so I don't know when it actually started.

I don't have a reproducible example but it's happening 10~20 times a day across our prod environment, and I can deploy a custom version / build with a custom Go if needed to collect information.

I checked all Go memstats metrics and some host metrics of an occurence and didn't see anything remarkable, it was not that close to the memory limits, not allocating that much, not using a lot of CPU, etc.

I'm pretty sure we don't set GOMEMLIMIT, we set GOGC to 50, and one occurence had GOMAXPROCS set to 21 (but that can vary a lot depending on the host).

If it changes anything the service is privileged and uses eBPF for system monitoring. The entrypoint is defined [here](https://github.com/DataDog/datadog-agent/blob/main/cmd/system-probe/main.go), but it's not a trivial binary.

### What did you see happen?

Here is the start of a stack trace of an occurrence, I can share the full stack trace if it's useful.

<details>
<summary>Stacktrace</summary>

```
runtime: full=0x600467c8000067 next=1153 jobs=1144 nDataRoots=2 nBSSRoots=22 nSpanRoots=64 nStackRoots=1053 spanq.n=0
panic: non-empty mark queue after concurrent mark
fatal error: panic on system stack
runtime stack:
runtime.throw({0x2890e61?, 0x2c8be90?})
	runtime/panic.go:1094 +0x48 fp=0x74aa367fbba0 sp=0x74aa367fbb70 pc=0x48b008

	runtime/mgc.go:1633 +0x409 fp=0x74aa367fbcc8 sp=0x74aa367fbc50 pc=0x4327a9
runtime.gcMarkTermination.func1()
	runtime/mgc.go:1059 +0x17 fp=0x74aa367fbce0 sp=0x74aa367fbcc8 pc=0x431b57
runtime.systemstack(0x800000)
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:513 +0x4a fp=0x74aa367fbcf0 sp=0x74aa367fbce0 pc=0x491d0a
goroutine 29 gp=0xc0003936c0 m=71 mp=0xc004181808 [garbage collection]:
runtime.systemstack_switch()
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:478 +0x8 fp=0xc003f1fa28 sp=0xc003f1fa18 pc=0x491ca8

runtime.gcMarkDone()
	runtime/mgc.go:1032 +0x369 fp=0xc003f1ff38 sp=0xc003f1fe98 pc=0x4306a9

	runtime/mgc.go:1373 +0x25 fp=0xc003f1ffe0 sp=0xc003f1ffc8 pc=0x431d05
runtime.goexit({})
	runtime/asm_amd64.s:1693 +0x1 fp=0xc003f1ffe8 sp=0xc003f1ffe0 pc=0x493cc1
```

comment.body:
Green Tea shouldn't have anything to do with this. Trying with Go 1.26 RC2 is not a bad idea, but I suspect nothing will be different. (Well... the mark termination conditions changed _slightly_, but everything still follows the same fallback paths. To not take those fallback paths and end up here anyway requires a very specific set of circumstances...)

This is about mark termination (which is hard, subtle, and we know has problems, see #27993, which has had a workaround for years and no known crashes since), and the fundamentals haven't been touched in a _long_ time, except for a relatively recent tweak (I think around Go 1.24) to support the `weak` package.

Do you know if `weak.Pointer` is used frequently in your codebase? That's my only guess.

Happy to hear any better suggestions.

In this case, exclude pattern could have much larger range and some specific important packages in that range will not be excluded. This may easier exclude patterns for large local mirror.

Happy to hear any better suggestions.

issue.title: use libsodium
issue.body:
将 token 换成 libsodium 生成的签名（含消息明文）的十六进制表示。好处：
- 避免和难用且危险的 openssl 打交道
- 不用搞无意义的 cert，只需要一对公私钥
- 不用手工拆解 id 和签名
- token 验证逻辑在其他语言中更易于实现，避免出错
- 避免选手因为 base64 字符问题被坑
- token 是确定性的，一个人只对应一个 token

comment.body: Let's try with libsodium this year -- AFAIK geekgame 2025 is using this patch.

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -12,32 +12,103 @@ FreeBSD 预编译软件包的**动态缓存**
 
 所有受官方支持版本的 amd64 和 aarch64 架构，详细参见 <https://pkg.freebsd.org/>。
 
-仓库包括季度分支 quarterly 和滚动更新的 latest 仓库。
+仓库提供两种软件包分支：季度更新的 quarterly 与滚动更新的 latest。两者仅影响第三方软件包（ports）。
 
 !!! tip
 
-    并非所有版本和架构都同时拥有 quarterly 或 latest 仓库，如 CURRENT 仅有 latest。
+    软件包分支与 FreeBSD 基本系统版本无关。不同 RELEASE / STABLE / CURRENT 均可使用 quarterly 或 latest。
 
-## 使用方法
+    但并非所有版本和架构都同时提供两种分支，例如 CURRENT 通常仅提供 latest。
 
-!!! warning
+    从 FreeBSD 15.0 起，若在安装系统时或事后手动启用了 pkgbase，则基本系统组件也会以软件包形式由 pkg 管理。此时，`/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos`目录下将会默认带有一个配置文件。
 
-    在操作前请做好相应备份。
+    在这种情况下，请先检查 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos` 是否为空，并确认 `/usr/local/etc/pkg` 下不存在旧配置文件；如存在请先备份。
 
-为了避免可能出现的向后兼容问题，基本系统中未预置真实的 pkg(8) 工具，需要在线安装。参见 [man pkg(7)](https://man.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=pkg)。安装方法为直接输入命令 `pkg` 根据提示进行确认安装。为了避免因网络问题造成安装失败，建议先按以下方法换源后再安装 pkg。
+    请勿将文件命名为其他 `.conf` 以外的名称，避免覆盖现有设置。
+    
+    完成确认后，再写入对应的镜像配置到 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/ustc.conf`。
 
-FreeBSD pkg 包管理器的官方源的配置路径为 `/etc/pkg/FreeBSD.conf`。不建议直接修改此文件：该配置文件是 FreeBSD 基本系统的一部分，会随着基本系统的更新而变化。
+## 使用方法
 
-应创建路径及文件 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/USTC.conf` 来覆盖配置，文件内容如下：
+!!! warning
 
-```yaml
-ustc: { 
-  url: "https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/${ABI}/quarterly"
-}
-FreeBSD: { enabled: no }
-```
+    在操作前请做好相应备份。
 
-若要使用滚动更新的 latest 仓库，将 `url` 这行配置末尾的 `quarterly` 换成 `latest` 即可。
+    此处只列出了 `quaterly` 季度分支，若有其他需求，请自行修改。
+
+    为了避免可能出现的向后兼容问题，基本系统中未预置真实的 pkg(8) 工具，需要在线安装。参见 [man pkg(7)](https://man.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=pkg)。安装方法为直接输入命令 `pkg` 根据提示进行确认安装。为了避免因网络问题造成安装失败，建议先按以下方法换源后再安装 pkg。
+
+    FreeBSD pkg 包管理器的官方源的配置路径为 `/etc/pkg/FreeBSD.conf`。不建议直接修改此文件：该配置文件是 FreeBSD 基本系统的一部分，会随着基本系统的更新而变化。
+    应新创建路径及文件 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/ustc.conf` 来覆盖配置，文件内容如下：
+
+=== "FreeBSD 15.X-RELEASE"
+
+    ```yaml
+    ustc-ports: { 
+        url: "https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/${ABI}/quarterly",
+        mirror_type: "none",
+        signature_type: "fingerprints",
+        fingerprints: "/usr/share/keys/pkg",
+        enabled: yes
+    }
+
+    ustc-ports-kmods: {

-    并非所有版本和架构都同时拥有 quarterly 或 latest 仓库，如 CURRENT 仅有 latest。
+    软件包分支与 FreeBSD 基本系统版本无关。不同 RELEASE / STABLE / CURRENT 均可使用 quarterly 或 latest。
 
-## 使用方法
+    但并非所有版本和架构都同时提供两种分支，例如 CURRENT 通常仅提供 latest。
 
-!!! warning
+    从 FreeBSD 15.0 起，若在安装系统时或事后手动启用了 pkgbase，则基本系统组件也会以软件包形式由 pkg 管理。此时，`/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos`目录下将会默认带有一个配置文件。
 
-    在操作前请做好相应备份。
+    在这种情况下，请先检查 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos` 是否为空，并确认 `/usr/local/etc/pkg` 下不存在旧配置文件；如存在请先备份。
 
-为了避免可能出现的向后兼容问题，基本系统中未预置真实的 pkg(8) 工具，需要在线安装。参见 [man pkg(7)](https://man.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=pkg)。安装方法为直接输入命令 `pkg` 根据提示进行确认安装。为了避免因网络问题造成安装失败，建议先按以下方法换源后再安装 pkg。
+    请勿将文件命名为其他 `.conf` 以外的名称，避免覆盖现有设置。
+    
+    完成确认后，再写入对应的镜像配置到 `/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/ustc.conf`。
comment.body: 如果可以的话，最好保持文件名和原来的帮助一致（`USTC.conf`）

-仓库包括季度分支 quarterly 和滚动更新的 latest 仓库。
+仓库提供两种软件包分支：季度更新的 quarterly 与滚动更新的 latest。两者仅影响第三方软件包（ports）。
 
 !!! tip
 
-    并非所有版本和架构都同时拥有 quarterly 或 latest 仓库，如 CURRENT 仅有 latest。

-!!! warning
+    从 FreeBSD 15.0 起，若在安装系统时或事后手动启用了 pkgbase，则基本系统组件也会以软件包形式由 pkg 管理。此时，`/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos`目录下将会默认带有一个配置文件。
comment.body:
```suggestion
    从 FreeBSD 15.0 起，若在安装系统时或事后手动启用了 pkgbase，则基本系统组件也会以软件包形式由 pkg 管理。此时，`/usr/local/etc/pkg/repos` 目录下将会默认带有一个配置文件。
```

issue.title: Clarify FreeBSD 15 pkg repository rename to FreeBSD-ports
issue.body:
Starting from FreeBSD 15, the default pkg repository name has changed from `FreeBSD` to `FreeBSD-ports`. Disabling `FreeBSD: { enabled: no }` no longer works, because pkg still enables `FreeBSD-ports` by default.

This behavior is defined in FreeBSD 15 source code:

* Default repo config: https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd-src/blob/releng/15.0/usr.sbin/pkg/FreeBSD.conf.quarterly

* Commit introducing the change: https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd-src/commit/c83705a5756ef2b01e0e5b1430e8c5548d4cca6e

Currently, this rename is easy to miss and causes confusion, especially when configuring third-party or regional mirrors. I suggest documenting this change clearly, or updating examples to explicitly mention that `FreeBSD-ports` must be disabled instead of `FreeBSD` on FreeBSD 15 and later.

This PR is intended as a documentation clarification only.

refer: https://blog.mxdyeah.com/post/freebsd-15-pkg-repo-freebsd-ports-trap

comment.body:
> 已经修改，请审阅。

ports-kmods 和 base 的部分修改似乎没有体现。

issue.body:
### 先决条件 (Prerequisites)

- [x] 我已确认这个镜像源从未在 [其他 issues](https://github.com/ustclug/mirrorrequest/issues) 中讨论过。

### 项目介绍 (Project introduction)

UV是由Astral公司（Rust工具Ruff的开发者）推出的高性能Python包管理工具，基于Rust编写，旨在替代传统的pip和pip-tools。其核心优势在于极快的速度（比pip快10-100倍）、轻量级设计（仅几十MB）以及现代化的依赖管理（支持pyproject.toml和uv.lock文件）。UV集成了虚拟环境管理、Python版本控制、依赖解析等功能，目标是成为类似Rust Cargo的全能工具，简化Python开发流程。

uv本身提供了下载镜像参数UV_PYTHON_INSTALL_MIRROR，文档可见https://docs.astral.sh/uv/configuration/environment/#uv_python_install_mirror，希望添加ustc镜像源加速下载

### 上游地址与镜像方法 (Upstream & mirroring method)

https://github.com/astral-sh/python-build-standalone
可参考南京大学镜像站的同步方式：https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/list/python-build-standalone
https://mirror.nju.edu.cn/github-release/indygreg/python-build-standalone/

### 镜像大小 (Mirror size)

~17GB

_No response_

This behavior is defined in FreeBSD 15 source code:

* Commit introducing the change: https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd-src/commit/c83705a5756ef2b01e0e5b1430e8c5548d4cca6e

Currently, this rename is easy to miss and causes confusion, especially when configuring third-party or regional mirrors. I suggest documenting this change clearly, or updating examples to explicitly mention that `FreeBSD-ports` must be disabled instead of `FreeBSD` on FreeBSD 15 and later.

issue.title: `mirrors.cernet.edu.cn` 上的 CTAN 镜像访问返回 404
issue.body:
我尝试访问 CERNET 的 CTAN 镜像 [https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN](https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN)，但页面显示“404 Page Not Found”，这导致我无法通过该镜像更新。

- 问题发生时间：今天（2026年2月2日）10:00。
- 如何复现：在任何浏览器中直接打开上述链接。
- 预期结果：正常显示 CTAN 镜像跳转。
- 实际结果：服务器返回 404 错误页面。

我测试了其他国内镜像（如中科大、清华大学的），它们均工作正常。
我曾使用 tlmgr 将该镜像设置为默认源并更新，相关命令如下：
```
tlmgr option repository https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN/systems/texlive/tlnet
tlmgr update --list
```
这是我的 tlmgr 更新时遇到的错误日志：
```
Unable to download the checksum of the remote TeX Live database,
but found a local copy, so using that.

You may want to try specifying an explicit or different CTAN mirror,
or maybe you need to specify proxy information if you're behind a firewall;
see the information and examples for the -repository option at
https://tug.org/texlive/doc/install-tl.html
(and in the output of install-tl --help).

tlmgr.pl: package repository https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN/systems/texlive/tlnet (not verified: unknown)
tlmgr.pl: would save backups to C:/texlive/2025/tlpkg/backups
tlmgr.pl: no updates available
```
和
```
C:\texlive\2025\texmf-dist\scripts\texlive\tlmgr.pl: TLPDB::from_file could not get texlive.tlpdb from: https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN/systems/texlive/tlnet/tlpkg/texlive.tlpdb
Maybe the repository setting should be changed.
More info: https://tug.org/texlive/acquire.html
```

comment.body:
请提供以下命令的输出以帮助排查问题：

```pwsh
curl.exe -I -v https://mirrors.cernet.edu.cn/CTAN/systems/texlive/tlnet
```

issue.title: freebsd-pkg：部分软件包的校验和与上游不符合
issue.body:
### 问题描述 / Bug description

根据 <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/packagesite.txz>、<https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/data.txz>，以及实际在脚本中起判断作用的 <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/packagesite.txz>：

```ymal
{"name":"openjdk17","origin":"java/openjdk17","version":"17.0.16+8.1_4","comment":"Java Development Kit 17","maintainer":"java@FreeBSD.org","www":"https://openjdk.java.net/projects/jdk/17/","abi":"FreeBSD:14:amd64","arch":"freebsd:14:x86:64","prefix":"/usr/local","sum":"bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f","flatsize":322603597,"path":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","repopath":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","licenselogic":"single","licenses":["GPLv2"],"pkgsize":165893853,"desc":"An open-source implementation of the Java Platform, Standard Edition,"
```
openjdk17 的 sha256 应为 `bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f`，但是下载的  <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/All/openjdk17-17.0.16%2B8.1_4.pkg> 校验和实为 `fbb69747ad18a7451d23278d33b91cf6f3de9b94ca87d0b16d2ca9aadbcfc9f0`

同时，使用官方源 pkg.freebsd.org 获取的 openjdk17 可以正常安装，其校验和同为  `bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f`：

```sh
# sha256 /var/cache/pkg/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg 
SHA256 (/var/cache/pkg/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg) = bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f
```

---

当前使用 USTC 报错如下：

```sh

FreeBSD repository is up to date.

158 MiB to be downloaded.

[2/2] Fetching openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg: 100%  158 MiB  55.3MB/s    00:03
```

```ymal
{"name":"openjdk17","origin":"java/openjdk17","version":"17.0.16+8.1_4","comment":"Java Development Kit 17","maintainer":"java@FreeBSD.org","www":"https://openjdk.java.net/projects/jdk/17/","abi":"FreeBSD:14:amd64","arch":"freebsd:14:x86:64","prefix":"/usr/local","sum":"bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f","flatsize":322603597,"path":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","repopath":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","licenselogic":"single","licenses":["GPLv2"],"pkgsize":165893853,"desc":"An open-source implementation of the Java Platform, Standard Edition,"
```

comment.body:
> > > > 有一个问题，当前的版本跟踪是按包跟踪的，而不是按文件跟踪的，这导致程序无法单独跟踪 metadata 的状态。即使可以跟踪 metadata 的状态，PyPI 也没法单独跟踪 metadata 的变更。
> > 
> > > > 我考虑以下的解决方案：
> > 
> > > > 
> > 
> > > > * 提供一个可以手动运行的指令，同步所有包文件的 metadata
> > 
> > > > * 对于尚未同步 metadata 的部分，Simple API 的 HTML/JSON 文件去掉 metadata 相关信息
> > 
> > > 
> > 
> > > 我看了一下代码，可以修改 skip_this_package 的实现吧。
> > 
> > 
> > 
> > 对于还没有更新到最新版本的镜像是可以的。但已经更新的镜像已对 Simple API 的 HTML/JSON 文件进行了修改，在相关的包有下一次更新前依然会保持在缺少 metadata 的状态。
> 
> 嗯，这些包只能 verify 或者手动 do_update

（我的考虑是目前的损坏是一个短期的状态，未来版本的 shadowmire 不会出现缺少 metadata 的状态，所以把会导致缺少的问题修好就足够了；已经出现问题的包也有办法处理）

forkee.name: lotus
forkee.description: A program analysis, verification, and optimization framework

release.name: Lotus v1.0
release.body:
**Release Date**: January 28, 2026

We are excited to announce the first stable release of **Lotus v1.0**, a comprehensive program analysis, verification, and optimization framework built on LLVM. This release represents a major milestone with a mature, production-ready codebase that has been validated through multiple research publications and industrial applications.

## Overview

Lotus v1.0 provides a versatile and industrial-scale program analysis framework that combines multiple analysis techniques to detect security vulnerabilities, concurrency bugs, and numerical errors in C/C++ programs. The framework has been tested on x86/ARM Linux and ARM Mac using LLVM-14 with Z3.

## Major Features

### 🐛 Comprehensive Bug Detection Framework

Lotus v1.0 includes a unified bug detection framework with four major checker categories:

#### 1. **Pulse Checker** (NEW in v1.0)
- **Biabductive Analysis**: Path-sensitive interprocedural analysis inspired by Infer's Pulse
- **Memory Safety Detection**: 
  - Use-after-free (CWE-416)
  - Null pointer dereference (CWE-476)
  - Uninitialized read (CWE-457)
- **Advanced Features**:
  - Disjunctive domain for path-sensitive reasoning
  - Loop abstraction with widening and invariant inference
  - Function summaries for scalable interprocedural analysis
  - Taint analysis integration for security vulnerabilities
  - Latent issue tracking

#### 2. **GVFA (Global Value Flow Analysis) Checkers**
- Null pointer dereference detection
- Use-after-free detection
- Uninitialized variable use detection
- Invalid free() detection
- Stack address misuse detection

#### 3. **KINT Numerical Checkers**
- Integer overflow detection (signed and unsigned)
- Division by zero detection
- Bad shift detection
- Array out-of-bounds detection
- Dead branch detection
- Range analysis with SMT solving

#### 4. **Concurrency Checkers**
- Data race detection using MHP (May Happen in Parallel) analysis
- Deadlock detection using lock set analysis
- Atomicity violation detection
- Enhanced thread handling and static vector clock analysis

### 🔍 Advanced Alias Analysis

Lotus provides multiple state-of-the-art alias analysis algorithms:

| Analysis | Tool/Command | Analysis Type | Flow-Sensitive | Context-Sensitive | Field-Sensitive | Notes |
|----------|--------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-------|
| **SparrowAA** | `sparrow-aa` | Inclusion-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (CI, 1-CFA, 2-CFA) | ❌ No | More graph simplification algorithms; no on-the-fly callgraph construction |
| **AserPTA** | `aser-aa` | Inclusion-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (CI, 1-CFA, 2-CFA, Origin) | ✅ Yes | On-the-fly callgraph construction; supports both field-insensitive and field-sensitive modes |
| **CFLAA** | - | - | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | From LLVM |
| **DyckAA** | - | Unification-based | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | - |
| **Dynamic** | - | Dynamic | - | - | - | Runtime analysis |
| **FPA** | `fpa` | Type-based | - | - | - | Function pointer analysis |
| **LotusAA** | `lotus-aa` | Inclusion-based | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | - | Flow- and context-sensitive |
| **seadsa** | `sea-dsa-dg`, `seadsa-tool` | Unification-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | - | Context-sensitive heap (heap cloning) |
| **SRAA** | - | Range-based | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | Flow- and context-insensitive |
| **UnderApproxAA** | - | Pattern-based | - | - | - | Must-alias analysis |
| **AllocAA** | - | - | - | - | - | - |
| **TPA** | `tpa` | Inclusion-based | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (k-limiting) | - | Flow- and context-sensitive with k-limiting |
### 📊 Intermediate Representations

- **PDG (Program Dependence Graph)**: Comprehensive program representation with Cypher query support
- **SVFG (Sparse Value Flow Graph)**: Efficient value flow representation
- **DyckVFG**: Context-sensitive value flow graph
- **Thin Slicing**: Advanced slicing capabilities based on EECS-2006-184

### 🧮 Constraint Solving & Data Flow

- **SMT Solving**: Z3 integration for path-sensitive verification
- **BDD (CUDD)**: Binary decision diagrams for efficient constraint solving
- **WPDS**: Weighted pushdown systems for interprocedural analysis
- **IFDS/IDE Framework**: Interprocedural finite distributive subset / interprocedural distributive environment framework
- **Taint Analysis**: Information flow tracking for security vulnerabilities

### 🔧 Verification & Optimization

- **CLAM**: Abstract interpretation-based verification
- **Seahorn**: Model checking and verification
- **Symbolic Abstraction**: Symbolic execution capabilities
- **Failure-Directed Trimming**: LLVM pass for failure-directed program trimming
- **Loop Invariant Analysis**: Automatic loop invariant inference
- **Function Invariant Analysis**: Function-level invariant analysis

### 📝 Enhanced Reporting & Integration

- **SARIF 2.1.0 Support**: Standard format for static analysis results
- **JSON Output**: Machine-readable bug reports
- **Bug Suppression Management**: Flexible suppression system for known issues
- **Centralized Bug Reporting**: Unified `BugReportMgr` for all checkers
- **Rich Diagnostics**: Detailed traces and source location information
- **CWE Mapping**: Common Weakness Enumeration mappings for all bug types

## Command-Line Tools

Lotus v1.0 provides a comprehensive set of command-line tools:

### Bug Detection Tools
- `lotus-pulse` - Pulse biabductive analysis checker
- `lotus-gvfa` - Global value flow analysis vulnerability checker
- `lotus-kint` - Integer and numerical bug detection
- `lotus-concur` - Concurrency bug detection
- `lotus-taint` - Taint analysis tool

### Alias Analysis Tools
- `lotus-aa` - flow- and context-sensitive analysis
- `dyck-aa` - Dyck-CFL reachability alias analysis
- `aser-aa` - Aser points-to analysis
- `sparrow-aa` - Sparrow points-to analysis
- `fpa` - Function pointer analysis
- `sea-dsa-dg` - Sea-DSA analysis
- `dynaa` - Dynamic alias analysis validation
-  etc.

### Analysis & Query Tools
- `pdg-query` - PDG querying with Cypher support
- `lotus-opt` - Optimization passes
- `clam` - Abstract interpretation verification
- `seahorn` - Model checking

### Solver Tools
- `owl` - SMT solver interface
- `slot` - Constraint solving
- `staub` - Additional solver utilities

## Platform Support

- **Operating Systems**: x86 Linux, ARM Linux, ARM Mac (Apple Silicon)
- **LLVM**: 14.x
- **CMake**: 3.10+
- **Compiler**: C++14 compatible compiler

## Documentation

Comprehensive documentation is available at:
- **Online Docs**: https://zju-pl.github.io/lotus
- **User Guide**: Installation, tutorials, architecture overview
- **API Reference**: Complete developer documentation
- **Examples**: Extensive example code and benchmarks

## Research Impact

Lotus has been used in multiple high-impact research publications:

- **TOSEM 2026**: Compiler Optimizations-Based SMT Simplifications
- **ISSTA 2025**: Program Analysis Combining Generalized Bit-Level and Word-Level Abstractions
- **S&P 2024**: Titan: Efficient Multi-target Directed Greybox Fuzzing
- **USENIX Security 2024**: Type-Based Call Graph Construction with Regional Pointer Information
- **TSE 2024**: Fast and Precise Static Null Exception Analysis
- **OOPSLA 2022**: Indexing the Extended Dyck-CFL Reachability

## Installation

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus
cd lotus
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..
make -j$(nproc)
```

For custom LLVM installations:
```bash
cmake .. -DLLVM_BUILD_PATH=/path/to/llvm/lib/cmake/llvm
```

## Quick Start

### Detect Memory Safety Bugs
```bash
# Using Pulse checker
./build/bin/lotus-pulse program.bc

# Using GVFA checker
./build/bin/lotus-gvfa --vuln-type=nullpointer program.bc
```

### Detect Integer Bugs
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-kint --check-all program.bc
```

### Detect Concurrency Bugs
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-concur --check-data-races --check-deadlocks program.bc
```

### Generate SARIF Report
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-pulse --sarif-output=report.sarif program.bc
```

## What's New in v1.0

### Major Additions
- **Pulse Checker**: Complete biabductive analysis framework for memory safety
- **SARIF Support**: Industry-standard reporting format (SARIF 2.1.0)
- **Bug Suppression System**: Flexible suppression management
- **Cypher Query Support**: Query PDG using Cypher language
- **Loop/Function Invariant Analysis**: Automatic invariant inference
- **Failure-Directed Trimming**: LLVM pass for program trimming
- **Enhanced Concurrency Analysis**: Improved MHP and lock set analysis

### Improvements
- Unified bug reporting system across all checkers
- Enhanced diagnostic messages with detailed traces
- Better integration between analysis components
- Improved documentation and examples
- Performance optimizations across multiple components

## Breaking Changes

None. This is the first stable release.

## Known Limitations

- LLVM 14.x is required (automatic download not yet implemented)
- Z3 4.11 is required (automatic download not yet implemented)
- Some advanced features may require additional configuration

## Contributors

We thank all contributors who made this release possible:

- rainoftime / cutelimination
- qingkaishi
- rhuab
- Zahrinas
- Rexxar-Jack-Remar


## Support & Community

- **GitHub**: https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus
- **Documentation**: https://zju-pl.github.io/lotus
- **Issues**: Please report bugs and feature requests on GitHub

## Future Roadmap

- Support for additional LLVM versions
- Automatic dependency management
- Enhanced performance optimizations
- Additional bug detection capabilities
- Improved IDE integration

---

**Thank you for using Lotus!** We hope this framework helps advance your program analysis research and development.

## What's Changed

issue.title: Add a simple linear DMA engine component (SocDma)
issue.body:
This is motivated by @dangfan. We would like to introduce DMA I/O in our computer organization textbook, but currently logisim-evolution does not have DMA support.

## Motivation

CPU-driven memcpy to the VGA framebuffer is slow (~5 ticks per word). This DMA engine
can transfer 16 (by default) words/tick by default (~80× faster), making VGA image updates practical in
simulation. The three-bus design also enables flexible SoC topologies where data paths are
separated from control paths.

## Summary

- Add a new `SocDma` component to the SoC library: a clock-driven, linear memory-copy DMA engine
  with configurable burst size (power-of-2, 1–256 words/tick), MMIO control/status registers
  (SRC_ADDR, DST_ADDR, LENGTH, CONTROL, STATUS, BYTES_DONE), optional completion interrupt,
  and three independent bus connections (control slave, source read master, destination write master)
  that allow cross-bus DMA transfers.
- Extend `SocSimulationManager.registerComponent()` to propagate the simulation manager to any
  additional `SocBusInfo`-typed attributes beyond the standard `SOC_BUS_SELECT`, enabling
  components with multiple bus connections.
- Add English & Chinese HTML documentation (`socdma.html`) with register map, pin descriptions, attribute
  reference, and a VGA usage guide.

Note: I designed the specific behavior and interfaces of this component. Some detailed implementation and documentation was generated by AI and has been reviewed by me. I compiled and tested the component manually.

comment.body: @BFH-ktt1 I've fixed most of copilot review comments, except the one for missing icons -- I am not able to draw an icon of the similar style, and using `empty.png` looks weird too. So I just put the "DMA" as the icon. I think it looks acceptable for now.

## Test Plan

## Test Result

<details>
<summary> Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist </summary>

- [ ] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)".
- [ ] The test plan, such as providing test command.
- [ ] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results

</details>

comment.body:
> > Typically the wheels get built within 30 minutes since the commit appears on master.
> 
> But main branch is quite active, there is usually 2~3 PRs merged per hour. During waiting the 30min gap, there can be another PR merged in, making the 404 error occurred consistently.

This is true -- but I don't think it is necessary to rebase onto the latest master as soon as a new PR is merged?

> > In addition, when in a real urgent case, the user can always specify a commit by hand, of which wheels are already built and safe to use.
> 
> Yea, we can specify a commit by hand. I just found that I can never install the wheel through `VLLM_USE_PRECOMPILED=1` recently to get kernels update, and have to manually set `VLLM_PRECOMPILED_WHEEL_COMMIT` or run full build. 😓

If what you want are up-to-date "kernel updates", then you should use the _actual_ latest commit, not the `nightly` index, which might not contain the latest compilation result of native code and may not be compatible.

comment.body:
I'm somewhat hesitant to do some because:

* It's not quite safe, even with the warning (which the user might most probably ignore).
* Typically the wheels get built within 30 minutes since the commit appears on master. 

I have implemented some retry logic in CI steps to avoid failure (#30781). But for actual human use, I think waiting should be okay. In addition, when in a real urgent case, the user can always specify a commit by hand, of which wheels are already built and safe to use.

issue.title: build: change target python platform tag to `cp38-abi3`
issue.body:
## Purpose

Currently the wheel is built for `cp39-cp39`, limiting the python version that could use the package.
This PR changes the target to [`cp38-abi3`](https://github.com/PyO3/setuptools-rust/blob/main/docs/building_wheels.md#building-for-abi3), so that any python version after 3.8 can use it.

Additionally, it also:

* removes some generated files that should not be cached
* extracts the build script in the release pipeline to avoid duplication
* fixes some deprecation and aligns the versions in code

## Test Plan

Wheel building is tested locally. Buildkite config changes will be tested by CI.

## Test Result

See [CI results](https://buildkite.com/vllm/router/builds/216/steps/canvas?sid=019c320d-aef4-4c1e-8d9c-473fe6b9e3f0&tab=output):

```
[2026-02-06T08:31:33Z] Successfully built vllm_router-0.1.10.tar.gz and vllm_router-0.1.10-cp38-abi3-linux_x86_64.whl
```

---
<details>
<summary> Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist </summary>

- [x] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)".
- [x] The test plan, such as providing test command.
- [x] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,22 @@
+#!/bin/bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+# Install system dependencies (manylinux_2_28 uses dnf)
+dnf install -y openssl-devel protobuf-compiler protobuf-devel
+
+# Install Rust toolchain
+curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
+source $HOME/.cargo/env
+
+# Build Rust binary for release
+cargo build --release
+
+# Build Python wheels using Python 3.9 (available in manylinux image)
+/opt/python/cp39-cp39/bin/pip install -U pip setuptools wheel setuptools-rust build auditwheel
+/opt/python/cp39-cp39/bin/python -m build
+
+# Repair wheel to have proper manylinux tags
+/opt/python/cp39-cp39/bin/auditwheel repair dist/*.whl -w dist/
+# Remove the original non-manylinux wheel
+rm dist/*-linux_*.whl || true
comment.body: I think it is safe to do so.

Running in Nightly CI

Yes, runtime/pprof uses `SIGPROF`.

If there is no non-Go code involved in this program, then this should be impossible. The Go runtime always installs signal handlers with `SA_ONSTACK` set. Is this problem reproducible? Is there a way that we can reproduce it ourselves?

comment.body:
The runtime error message is trying to tell you the problem: "fatal error: non-Go code disabled sigaltstack".

Quoting https://pkg.go.dev/os/signal:

> If the non-Go code installs any signal handlers, it must use the SA_ONSTACK flag with sigaction. Failing to do so is likely to cause the program to crash if the signal is received. Go programs routinely run with a limited stack, and therefore set up an alternate signal stack.

In this case the signal is `SIGPROF`. Look for non-Go code linked into your program that is setting up a signal handler for `SIGPROF`.

issue.title: proposal: math/bits: add function for overflow-safe multiply-and-add

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
### Proposal Details

I was trying to write overflow safe code today. Not only did that take a lot longer than I hoped, I'm also not super confident I got it correct. It requires a better understanding of two's complement arithmetic than I have, to be honest.

There are multiple proposals to make it easier, among them #30613 and a bunch of closed issues linked from there. Most of those are language changes, though. I would suggest a lower friction solution by adding an intrinsic to `math/bits`:

```go
// MulAndAdd returns a+b*c, but checks for overflows. If either the multiplication or the addition
// over- or underflow, the value of res is undefined and ok is false.
func MulAndAdd[T intType](a, b, c T) (res T, ok bool)
```

(name could probably be better)

The advantage of an intrinsic over [libraries that help with this](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/g-utils/overflow) is

1. more confidence in the implementation
2. the compiler can use type-dependent checks. e.g. signed types can use different checks than unsigned types, or it could use derived bounds on the inputs to completely eliminate it (e.g. if it can prove that both arguments are less than `MaxInt16`, their sum can not overflow `uint16`)
3. the compiler can special-case constant cases like `a == 0`, or `a,b∈{0,1}`
4. it can generate appropriate conditional jumps/moves on the overflow-flag, for essentially zero overhead

The API covers both addition and multiplication using appropriate constants. When decoding binary data it is common to use `a` as an offset, `b` as an element size and `c` as a number of elements. #20757 proposes APIs to make `time.Duration` safer - with this, e.g. `bits.MulAndAdd(a, b, time.Hour)` would be a safe way to add `b` hours to `a`.

Conversion and negation are also operations that are very common to need overflow-checks, but they are relatively easy to implement: `-x` can only overflow if `x` is `MinIntN` and for conversion, `A(B(x)) == x` ensures that `var x A` can be converted to `B` without loss ([edit] actually, this fails for conversions between signed and unsigned, which TBH kind of proves my point [/edit]).

[g-utils/overflow](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/g-utils/overflow) also has functions for division. Personally, I can't remember ever needing those in a context where I was worried about overflows. If people need them, we can consider adding something for those as well.

---

TBH I'm not 100% convinced this is a good idea. But I'm very frustrated how hard it is to write overflow-safe code *even if you are careful*. And I would really like us to do *something* to make that easier.

comment.body: The general concept makes sense to me but why multiply-and-add in particular? There are plenty of other operations.

issue.title: encoding/base32: decoder missing length error check with NoPadding

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.23.5 linux/amd64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
GO111MODULE=''
GOARCH='amd64'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/home/user/.cache/go-build'
GOENV='/home/user/.config/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFLAGS=''
GOHOSTARCH='amd64'
GOHOSTOS='linux'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMODCACHE='/home/user/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='linux'
GOPATH='/home/user/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='direct'
GOROOT='/usr/lib/go-1.23'
GOSUMDB='off'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='local'
GOTOOLDIR='/usr/lib/go-1.23/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.23.5'
GODEBUG=''
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/home/user/.config/go/telemetry'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GOAMD64='v1'
AR='ar'
CC='gcc'
CXX='g++'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
GOMOD='/dev/null'
GOWORK=''
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -pthread -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build1974934359=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches'
```

### What did you do?

The `decode` function has [an error check](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.25.7/src/encoding/base32/base32.go#L343) for a final chunk whose length is is 1, 3, or 6. Any of these invalid lengths gives rise to a `CorruptInputError`. But the check only happens when padding is used: with `NoPadding`, the check is [skipped](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.25.7/src/encoding/base32/base32.go#L324). The invalid final chunk length `dlen` falls through and misses every case in the [subsequent `switch` statement](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.25.7/src/encoding/base32/base32.go#L362). The result is that the final chunk is silently ignored, and no error is reported.

Suppose that <var>X</var> is a valid base32-encoded input without padding. A nice property for the decoder to have is if adding characters to <var>X</var> (making it longer) has one of two effects: the output is longer (or at least no shorter), or an error is raised. Currently that is not the case: making the input longer can result in an output that is *shorter* by up to 3 bytes:

```
"ABCDEFGHIJKLM"  -> [00443214c74254b6]
"ABCDEFGHIJKLMN" -> [00443214c7]
```

This would be fine, if the would-be shorter decoding also returned an error. When padding is used, it does:

```
"ABCDEFGHIJKLM===" -> [00443214c74254b6]
"ABCDEFGHIJKLMN==" -> illegal base32 data at input byte 14
```

This is the program for the outputs below:
https://go.dev/play/p/s4WKAPqKTPE
It shows the error returns for input lengths from 0 to 8 with and without padding. The no-padding inputs cases return error, while the padding cases do for invalid lengths.

You might consider moving the length check into the [`switch dlen`](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.25.7/src/encoding/base32/base32.go#L362). I.e., do the `CorruptInputError` return for cases 1, 3, and 6. It was unexpected to me that execution could reach the `switch` and then none of the cases be matched.

Some past issues with base32 padding:

* #25296
* #25332
* #65166

### What did you see happen?

The error return for the no-padding cases is always `nil`. The padding cases detect invalid lengths and return a non-`nil` error.

```
0  ""          []            <nil>
0  ""          []            <nil>

1  "X"         []            <nil>
1  "X======="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 1

2  "XX"        [bd]          <nil>
2  "XX======"  [bd]          <nil>

3  "XXX"       []            <nil>
3  "XXX====="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 3

4  "XXXX"      [bdef]        <nil>
4  "XXXX===="  [bdef]        <nil>

5  "XXXXX"     [bdef7b]      <nil>
5  "XXXXX==="  [bdef7b]      <nil>

6  "XXXXXX"    []            <nil>
6  "XXXXXX=="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 6

7  "XXXXXXX"   [bdef7bde]    <nil>
7  "XXXXXXX="  [bdef7bde]    <nil>

8  "XXXXXXXX"  [bdef7bdef7]  <nil>
8  "XXXXXXXX"  [bdef7bdef7]  <nil>
```

### What did you expect to see?

The error returns for the no-padding cases should always match those of the padding cases.

```
0  ""          []            <nil>
0  ""          []            <nil>

1  "X"         []            illegal base32 data at input byte 1
1  "X======="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 1

2  "XX"        [bd]          <nil>
2  "XX======"  [bd]          <nil>

3  "XXX"       []            illegal base32 data at input byte 3
3  "XXX====="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 3

4  "XXXX"      [bdef]        <nil>
4  "XXXX===="  [bdef]        <nil>

5  "XXXXX"     [bdef7b]      <nil>
5  "XXXXX==="  [bdef7b]      <nil>

6  "XXXXXX"    []            illegal base32 data at input byte 6
6  "XXXXXX=="  []            illegal base32 data at input byte 6

7  "XXXXXXX"   [bdef7bde]    <nil>
7  "XXXXXXX="  [bdef7bde]    <nil>

8  "XXXXXXXX"  [bdef7bdef7]  <nil>
8  "XXXXXXXX"  [bdef7bdef7]  <nil>
```
